# Spatial Analysis Basics: Vector

> The strength of vector data lies in its ability to maintain precise boundaries and its capacity to handle topological relationships between spatial features. - Anonymous

::: callout-note
## Learning Objectives

Understand spatial relationship and how spatial relationship is implemented

Perform basic spatial queries

Conduct proximity analysis and vector overlay operations 

Understand and apply spatial join
:::

Spatial analysis combines information from many independent sources (layers) and derive new sets of information (results) by applying a sophisticated set of spatial operators @fig-multilayer. Spatial analysis is often considered as the most intriguing and remarkable aspect of modern GIS. Relying on spatial relationship among features, spatial analysis methods applied to vector data allow researchers and practitioners to extract meaningful patterns from geographic features, playing a crucial role in urban planning, environmental modeling, transportation networks, epidemiology, and many other fields. This chapter explores the basic spatial analysis techniques applied to vector data, detailing how *location* information is used to derive insights into spatial patterns.

![Spatial analysis combines information from multiple layers to generate new information. Image credit: University of Washington Libraries.](/figures/ch6/multilayer.png){#fig-multilayer}

## *Spatial relationship*

Spatial relationship describes how geographic features related to each other in space. There are several common spatial relationships, including the topological relationship (describes how features are connected regardless of their exact coordinates; see Chapter Vector data model), directional relationships (describe the relative direction of one feature to another), and distance relationships (describe how far features are from one another). Understanding spatial relationships is fundamental for many spatial analyses, including spatial queries, overlay operations and proximity analyses (discussed below).

One way to examine spatial relationships between features is using the Dimensionally Extended 9-Intersection Model (DE-9IM). In this model, it defines every spatial object (or feature) has an [*interior*]{.underline}, a [*boundary*]{.underline}, and an [*exterior*]{.underline} @fig-interiorexterior.

![Interior, boundary, and exterior for different spatial objects. For points, things are even stranger: the interior is the point; the boundary is the empty set and the exterior is everything else in the plane. Image credit: postgis.net.](/figures/ch6/interiorexterior.png){#fig-interiorexterior}

Depending on this definition, the spatial relationship between spatial objects can be derived by comparing the relationship between their interiors, boundaries, and exteriors. This systematic comparison produces an intersection matrix, commonly referred to as the Clementini Matrix, which can also be represented using a text string @fig-de9im. The matrix provides a precise way to describe spatial relationships such as overlap, containment, adjacency, or disjointedness, and it forms the basis for many topological analyses in GIS. Then, spatial query can be accomplished based on spatial relationship described by the DE-9IM pattern derived text string.

![Clementini matrix describes spatial relationship between two polygons. Image modified from postgis.net.](/figures/ch6/DE9IM.png){#fig-de9im}

## *Attribute queries*

Attribute queries utilize information saved in the attribute table associated with vector data layer, rather than the spatial location of each feature. Queries are built upon Structured Query Language (SQL) principles, because the attribute tables are essentially databases. Attribute queries allow select, filter or modify features based on their attributes @fig-selection.

![Select counties whose area is larger than 5,000 km2 and population is larger than 100,000 in the 2010 survey.](/figures/ch6/selection.png){#fig-selection}

## *Spatial queries*

Unlike attribute queries that work on attribute table, spatial queries utilize their location or spatial relationships between features or objects @fig-spatialquery. Note spatial queries can be combined with attribute queries (e.g., select all parks with area \> 10 ha within 1 km of a river).

![Select all the fire stations (blue) that are within 1 mile of hospitals (red).](/figures/ch6/spatialquery.png){#fig-spatialquery}

## *Proximity analysis*

Proximity analysis explores *what is near what.* It is a form of spatial query in which geographic features within a specified distance of a particular feature are selected. One method used to conduct the proximity analysis is called [*Buffer*]{.underline}, which defines a region that is less than or equal to a distance from a feature. It creates polygons around input features by a specified distance @fig-buffer. Buffer analysis can be applied to point, line and polygon layers.

![Buffers around points, lines, and polygons. Image credit: Saylor Academy.](/figures/ch6/buffer.png){#fig-buffer}

Another function that is often used with buffer analysis is called [*Dissolve*]{.underline}. Dissolve merges overlapping or adjacent buffers into a single region @fig-dissolve. Note that, by definition, [*Dissolve*]{.underline} is a vector operation that merges features based on shared attribute values or removes internal boundaries. Although it is often used with [*Buffer*]{.underline}, it is not only associated with [*Buffer*]{.underline}. [*Dissolve*]{.underline} is fundamentally an attribute driven aggregation operation.

![Buffer zones with dissolved (left) and with intact boundaries (right) showing overlapping areas. Image source: QGIS Documentation.](/figures/ch6/dissolve.png){#fig-dissolve}

## *Vector overlay operations*

Overlay operations superimpose multiple datasets (representing different themes) together to create new information by examining features overlap, combine, or differ @fig-overlay. Note that [*Clip*]{.underline}, which cuts out feature in the input layer (or function [*Clip Raster*]{.underline} works on raster data), works different from [*Intersect*]{.underline}. Using [*Clip*]{.underline}, the output layer from clip only has attribute from the input layer, while with [*intersect*]{.underline}, attributes from both layers are inherited to the output layer. 

![Overlay operations apply to two or multiple layers to combine, erase, modify or update features and generate a new layer. Image source: Saylor Academy.](/figures/ch6/overlay.png){#fig-overlay}

Function [*Merge*]{.underline} combines multiple input layers into one new single output without changing the features from the input datasets @fig-merge. All features from the input datasets will remain intact in the output data, even the features overlap.

![Spatial analysis methods (Dissolve, Merge, and Union) that combine features.](/figures/ch6/merge.png){#fig-merge}

## *Spatial Join*

Unlike the *Join* function (discussed in Chapter table and attribute operations) works on attribute tables, [*spatial join*]{.underline} uses ***spatial relationships*** to join ***attributes*** of features from one layer to another layer. For example, you have a polygon layer of school districts and a second layer of points, representing the location of schools. By running a spatial join, you can transfer the fields in the point attribute table on school information into the school district polygon layer.

Similar to the [*join*]{.underline} function, if multiple join features are found that fulfill the spatial relationship with a single target feature, instead of containing multiple copies of the same target feature, the attributes from the multiple join features will be aggregated using a field map merge rule (a summarized spatial join). For example, when multiple schools are found within a school district (which is often the case), the total number of students in the school district will be calculated by summing each school’s number of student.
